name: Short Film Shot List — Coverage Plan, Visualized

seo.title: Short Film Shot List Board
seo.description: A complete one-scene coverage plan: shot sizes with visual examples, a lettered shot list with lenses, the 180-degree rule diagrammed, and a copy-ready template — on one canvas.
seo.answer: A short film shot list is a numbered plan of every camera setup a scene needs — shot size, lens, angle, and movement — written before the shoot day. This board shows a complete seven-setup coverage plan for a two-person dialogue scene, plus the shot-size ladder, the 180-degree rule, and a copyable template.

faq.q: What order should you shoot a scene's coverage in?
faq.a: Shoot the master first, while performances and light are freshest, then work from wide shots to tight ones. Continuity, makeup, and energy all degrade over a shooting day, and they degrade most gracefully in that order. Inserts and cutaways go last because they forgive almost any mismatch in the edit.

Draw an imaginary line — the axis of action — between your two subjects and keep the camera on one side of it for the whole scene. This keeps screen direction constant and eyelines opposed, so the audience always knows who is looking at whom. Crossing the line mid-scene disorients viewers unless you bridge it with a neutral shot on the axis or a visible camera move.

faq.q: How many setups does a two-person dialogue scene need?
faq.a: Classic full coverage is five setups: a master, two over-the-shoulders, and two clean singles, plus any inserts or cutaways the scene calls for — seven is typical. On a small crew, each setup costs roughly 20 to 45 minutes of lighting, rehearsal, and takes, so a seven-setup scene is about half a shooting day.

faq.q: What lens is best for close-ups?
faq.a: On a full-frame camera, the classic close-up range is 65mm to 100mm; an 85mm flatters faces by compressing features and melting the background. A wide lens like 35mm can also work for close-ups, but it forces the camera physically near the actor, which reads as intimate or confrontational rather than neutral.

faq.q: What is the difference between a shot list and a storyboard?
faq.a: A shot list is a text inventory — shot number, size, lens, movement, and subject for every setup — and it doubles as the on-set scheduling document. A storyboard draws the actual frames. Most shorts get by with a shot list alone; storyboards earn their cost on complex action, stunts, and VFX sequences where exact framing must be agreed in advance.

seo.body: A working shot list for a two-person dialogue scene, built the way a director and DP would board it: the full shot-size ladder from extreme wide to extreme close-up with a visual example of each, a lettered coverage plan (master, over-the-shoulders, clean singles, inserts) with lens choices, a diagrammed 180-degree rule, and the craft notes that keep a set moving — shooting wide to tight and matching eyelines — plus a shot-list template you can copy column for column. Swap in your scene and walk on set with a plan. Made with Soleil Clusters.

header.sub: Two actors, seven setups, half a day

rows.what: Master — whole scene, both actors in frame
Clean single — ANA, from the turn to the exit
Insert — the unsent message on the phone

cellSpecs.text:
[#]A · Master · 25mm
Whole scene, both actors in frame. Shoot it first, while energy is highest.

[#]B · OTS on ANA · 50mm
Over SAM's shoulder, favoring ANA. Full scene, matched to 1C.

[#]C · OTS on SAM · 50mm
Over ANA's shoulder, favoring SAM. Mirror the framing of 1B.

image.alt: Two people in a tense kitchen conversation

cellSpecs.text:
[#]D · Single on ANA · 85mm
Clean single, from the turn to the exit. Hold after the last line.

[#]E · Single on SAM · 85mm
Clean single, full scene. Keep rolling for reactions.

[#]F · Insert · 100mm macro
The unsent message on the phone screen.

[#]G · Cutaway · 50mm
Kettle reaching boil. Forgives any cut.

cards.title: 1D and 1E — Clean singles

cards.html:
Coverage logic
Shoot the master first while energy and light are fresh, then work wide to tight — makeup, continuity, and performance all degrade gracefully in that order. Inserts and cutaways go last; they forgive everything.

header.sub: Two actors, one dashed line, and every camera position that works

cards.html:
The 180° rule
Draw a line between your two actors and keep the camera on one side of it. Eyelines stay opposed, screen direction stays constant, and the audience always knows who is looking at whom. The green arc is your side — every setup on this list lives there.

header.sub: What the glass and the dolly are saying

cards.html:
Lens language
24mm — context and energy, space stretches.
35mm — how the eye sees a room.
50mm — honest, neutral, documentary.
85mm — intimacy and compression; backgrounds melt.

Move with a reason
Static — observation. Push-in — realization. Handheld — instability. Dolly — grace and inevitability. If the move has no motivation, lock it off.

cards.title: Motivated movement
cards.caption: The camera is a character with opinions

header.sub: Copy the columns, swap in your scene, print it for the AD

lines.text: Shot list template — the columns that matter
One row per setup. Sort by location, then by lens — never by script order.
Shot number — scene number plus setup letter (1A, 1B…); keep letters stable once called on set.
Size — EWS, WS, MS, MCU, CU, ECU. The edit needs a ladder, not one size.
Lens — focal length states distance and feeling before the actors do.
Movement — static, pan, push-in, handheld, dolly. Name the motivation.
Subject and action — who is in frame and what must happen inside it.
Sound — sync dialogue, wild lines, and the room tone to grab before wrap.
Estimated time — 30 minutes per setup is honest on a small crew.
Mark the three shots the scene cannot live without, and protect them when the day runs long.
